content:
# Luke Chapter 17 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 109-110 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_017_page_001.jpg, LUK_017_page_002.jpg

---

**1.** And he said to his disciples, "It is impossible [for] the stumbling-blocks not to come, but woe [to him] through whom they come.

**2.** It would be better for him if a millstone were hung around his neck and he were thrown into the sea, than that he should cause-to-stumble one of these little-ones.

**3.** Take-heed to yourselves. If your brother sins, rebuke him, and if he repents, forgive him.

**4.** And if seven-times in the day he sins against you, and seven-times turns-back to you, saying, 'I repent,' you shall forgive him."

**5.** And the apostles said to the Lord, "Add faith to us."

**6.** And the Lord said, "If you have faith as a grain of mustard, you would say to this mulberry-tree, 'Be uprooted and be planted in the sea,' and it would obey you.

**7.** But which of you, having a slave plowing or shepherding, who, [when he] comes-in from the field, will say to him, 'Come at-once, recline [at table]'?

**8.** But will he not say to him, 'Prepare what I may eat-for-supper, and having girded-yourself, serve me until I eat and drink, and after these [things] you shall eat and drink'?

**9.** Does he have gratitude to the slave because he did the [things] commanded? I think not.

**10.** So also you, when you have done all the [things] commanded to you, say, 'We are unprofitable slaves; we have done [only] what we were owing to do.'"

**11.** And it came-to-be, in [his] going to Jerusalem, that he was passing-through [the] middle of Samaria and Galilee.

**12.** And [as] he was entering into a certain village, ten leprous men met [him], who stood at-a-distance,

**13.** and they lifted-up [their] voice, saying, "Jesus, Master, have-mercy-on us."

**14.** And having seen [them], he said to them, "Having gone, show yourselves to the priests." And it came-to-be, in their going, they were cleansed.

**15.** And one of them, having seen that he was healed, turned-back, with-a-loud voice glorifying God,

**16.** and he fell upon [his] face at his feet, giving-thanks to him; and he was a Samaritan.

**17.** And Jesus, answering, said, "Were not the ten cleansed? But the nine — where [are they]?

**18.** Were none found returning to give glory to God except this foreigner?"

**19.** And he said to him, "Having risen, go; your faith has saved you."

**20.** And having been asked by the Pharisees [as to] when the kingdom of God is coming, he answered them and said, "The kingdom of God does not come with observation,

**21.** nor will they say, 'Behold, here,' or 'There'; for behold, the kingdom of God is within you."

**22.** And he said to the disciples, "Days will come when you will desire to see one of the days of the Son of Man, and you will not see [it].

**23.** And they will say to you, 'Behold, there,' or 'Behold, here.' Do not go-away nor pursue [it].

**24.** For just-as the lightning, flashing from the [one part] under heaven, shines to the [other part] under heaven, so will the Son of Man be in his day.

**25.** But first it is necessary [for] him to suffer many [things] and to be rejected by this generation.

**26.** And just-as it came-to-be in the days of Noah, so will it be also in the days of the Son of Man:

**27.** they were eating, they were drinking, they were marrying, they were being-given-in-marriage, until [the] day Noah entered into the ark, and the flood came and destroyed all [of them].

**28.** Likewise, just-as it came-to-be in the days of Lot: they were eating, they were drinking, they were buying, they were selling, they were planting, they were building;

**29.** but [on the] day Lot went-out from Sodom, it rained fire and brimstone from heaven and destroyed all [of them].

**30.** According-to the same [things] it will be [on the] day the Son of Man is revealed.

**31.** In that day, the [one] who will be on the housetop, and his goods in the house, let him not come-down to take them up; and the [one] in the field, likewise let him not turn to the [things] behind.

**32.** Remember the wife of Lot.

**33.** Whoever seeks to preserve his soul will lose it, and whoever loses [it] will bring-it-to-life.

**34.** I say to you, in this night there will be two [men] upon one bed; the one will be taken-along and the other will be left.

**35.** There will be two [women] grinding together; the one will be taken-along and the other will be left."

**36.** [Verse 36 is absent from Codex Sinaiticus.]

**37.** And answering, they say to him, "Where, Lord?" And he said to them, "Where the body [is], there also the eagles will be gathered-together."

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **17:1 — "It is impossible [for] the stumbling-blocks not to come" (ανενδεκτον εστιν του τα σκανδαλα μη ελθειν):** Sinaiticus reads ανενδεκτον εστιν του τα σκανδαλα μη ελθειν. The word ανενδεκτον ("impossible/inadmissible") appears only here in the New Testament — a strong term indicating that stumbling-blocks (σκανδαλα) are an inevitable feature of the present age. The word σκανδαλον originally denoted the bait-stick of a trap — the trigger mechanism that ensnares. It thus describes anything that entices a person into sin or causes them to fall. The conjunction πλην ("but/nevertheless") introduces the solemn woe: the inevitability of stumbling-blocks does not excuse the one who sets them. The shift from chapter 16 is significant — Jesus has been addressing the Pharisees about wealth; now he turns to his disciples about communal responsibility.

2. **17:2 — "a millstone" (λιθος μυλικος):** Sinaiticus reads λιθος μυλικος. The adjective μυλικος ("of a mill") qualifies the stone as the heavy upper stone of a grinding mill — not the small hand-mill stone (μυλος) that a woman could turn, but the massive stone turned by a donkey (ονικος in Matthew 18:6). The phrase περικειται περι τον τραχηλον αυτου ("were hung around his neck") uses the perfect passive of περικειμαι ("to lie around/be placed around"), suggesting the stone is already in position, the drowning a foregone conclusion. The comparative λυσιτελει ("it would be better/more profitable") derives from λυσις + τελος ("releasing the end/paying the price") — a commercial term meaning "it pays better." The grim calculus: drowning with a millstone is a better investment than facing the consequences of causing a little-one to stumble.

3. **17:3-4 — "If your brother sins...seven-times" (εαν αμαρτη ο αδελφος σου...επτακις):** Sinaiticus reads εαν αμαρτη ο αδελφος σου, επιτιμησον αυτω, και εαν μετανοηση, αφες αυτω. The sequence is precise: sin triggers rebuke (επιτιμησον, aorist imperative — a definite, single act), repentance triggers forgiveness (αφες, aorist imperative — equally definite). The verb επιτιμαω ("to rebuke/warn") carries authority — the same word used for Jesus rebuking demons (4:35) and storms (8:24). The number "seven-times in the day" (επτακις της ημερας) does not set an upper limit but establishes a pattern of extravagant, seemingly unreasonable forgiveness. The verb επιστρεψη ("turns-back") is aorist subjunctive of επιστρεφω — a physical turning that embodies the inward turning of repentance. The obligation to forgive upon repentance is absolute and unlimited within the day's cycle.

4. **17:5 — "Add faith to us" (προσθες ημιν πιστιν):** Sinaiticus reads προσθες ημιν πιστιν. The verb προστιθημι ("to add/increase") is aorist imperative — a direct request, almost a demand. The apostles (αποστολοι, not merely "disciples" — Luke uses the more authoritative title here) respond to the command of unlimited forgiveness by recognizing their inadequacy. They do not ask for more patience, more wisdom, or more strength — they ask for more faith, intuiting that the capacity for radical forgiveness is not a moral achievement but a gift that must be received. The use of αποστολοι rather than μαθηται may signal that this teaching has particular relevance for those in positions of leadership and authority.

5. **17:6 — "as a grain of mustard" (ως κοκκον σιναπεως):** Sinaiticus reads ως κοκκον σιναπεως. The κοκκος σιναπεως ("grain of mustard") was proverbially the smallest seed known in Palestinian agriculture (cf. 13:19; Matthew 13:31-32). Jesus' response reframes the apostles' request: the issue is not the quantity of faith but its nature. Even faith the size of a mustard seed — faith that is genuine, living, however small — would command a συκαμινω ("mulberry-tree," the black mulberry, Morus nigra, known for its deep, tenacious root system) to uproot itself and be planted in the sea. The two aorist passive imperatives — εκριζωθητι ("be uprooted") and φυτευθητι ("be planted") — command the impossible: a deeply-rooted tree transplanted into an environment where it cannot survive. Faith's power is not proportional to its size but to the God in whom it trusts.

6. **17:7-10 — The parable of the unprofitable slave:** This parable is unique to Luke and follows directly from the teaching on faith. The rhetorical question in verse 7 expects the answer "none of you." The scenario is a small household with a single slave (δουλον) who both plows (αροτριωντα) and shepherds (ποιμαινοντα) — not a large estate but a modest farm. The verb διακονει μοι ("serve me") in verse 8 uses the present imperative — ongoing, sustained service. The verb περιζωσαμενος ("having girded-yourself") indicates tucking up the outer garment for active work — the posture of a servant. The question in verse 9, μη εχει χαριν τω δουλω ("does he have gratitude to the slave?"), expects a negative answer: no master thanks a slave for doing what is required. The application in verse 10 is striking: the word αχρειοι ("unprofitable/useless") does not mean the service is worthless but that it creates no surplus of merit — the slave has merely fulfilled his obligation (ο ωφειλομεν ποιησαι, "what we were owing to do"). This directly undercuts any theology of accumulated merit or surplus righteousness.

7. **17:11 — "through [the] middle of Samaria and Galilee" (δια μεσον Σαμαρειας και Γαλιλαιας):** Sinaiticus reads δια μεσον Σαμαρειας και Γαλιλαιας. The geographical note places Jesus on the border between Samaria and Galilee, traveling toward Jerusalem — a liminal space where the social and religious boundaries between Jews and Samaritans are physically proximate. The phrase δια μεσον ("through the middle/between") suggests he traveled along the border region, not through the heart of either territory. This setting is essential to the narrative: a border zone where a mixed group of lepers — Jewish and Samaritan — could coexist, united by their shared affliction across ethnic and religious lines.

8. **17:12 — "ten leprous men" (δεκα λεπροι ανδρες):** Sinaiticus reads δεκα λεπροι ανδρες. The number ten is the minimum required for a Jewish congregation (minyan), perhaps an intentional resonance. Levitical law required lepers to "dwell alone" outside the camp (Leviticus 13:46), and they formed communities of shared exile. The verb απηντησαν ("met [him]") indicates an intentional approach, but they "stood at-a-distance" (εστησαν πορρωθεν) in compliance with the law requiring lepers to maintain separation from the ritually clean (Leviticus 13:45-46). The detail ηραν φωνην ("they lifted-up [their] voice") uses the singular φωνην — one voice, unified in their cry, suggesting their affliction had dissolved the ethnic divisions that would normally separate Jew from Samaritan.

9. **17:14 — "Having gone, show yourselves to the priests" (πορευθεντες επιδειξατε εαυτους τοις ιερευσιν):** Sinaiticus reads πορευθεντες επιδειξατε εαυτους τοις ιερευσιν. Jesus commands them to follow the procedure of Leviticus 14:2-32 — presentation to the priest for inspection and declaration of cleanness — before they have been healed. This is a test of faith: they must act on the command while still leprous, trusting that the healing will come. The phrase εν τω υπαγειν αυτους ("in their going") indicates the healing occurred during the journey, not at the destination — obedience preceded and activated the miracle. The plural τοις ιερευσιν ("to the priests") is significant: the Jewish lepers would go to the temple priests in Jerusalem, but the Samaritan would go to the priests on Mount Gerizim. The command implicitly acknowledges and respects the Samaritan's distinct religious practice.

10. **17:16 — "and he was a Samaritan" (και αυτος ην Σαμαρειτης):** Sinaiticus reads και αυτος ην Σαμαρειτης. Luke places this ethnic identification last, as the climactic detail — a literary strategy of delayed revelation that forces the hearer to confront their assumptions. The Samaritan performs three acts: he "turned-back" (υπεστρεψεν) — abandoning his journey to the Gerizim priests; he glorifies God "with-a-loud voice" (μετα φωνης μεγαλης); and he "fell upon [his] face at his feet" (επεσεν επι προσωπον παρα τους ποδας αυτου) — full prostration, the posture of worship. The verb ευχαριστων ("giving-thanks") is the root of "Eucharist" — this is eucharistic gratitude, directed to Jesus as the source of divine healing. The Samaritan — the religious outsider, the heretic, the one whose priests and temple Jews rejected — is the one who recognizes where God is truly working.

11. **17:17-18 — "Were not the ten cleansed? But the nine — where [are they]?" (ουχι οι δεκα εκαθαρισθησαν; οι δε εννεα που;):** Sinaiticus reads ουχι οι δεκα εκαθαρισθησαν; οι δε εννεα που; Jesus' three questions form an ascending sequence of indictment: (1) Were not all ten cleansed? — establishing the fact. (2) Where are the nine? — exposing their absence. (3) Were none found returning to give glory to God except this αλλογενης ("foreigner/one of another race")? — the sharpest point. The word αλλογενης appears only here in the New Testament but is familiar from the LXX (e.g., the warning inscription on the temple barrier in Jerusalem used this word: "No foreigner [αλλογενης] may enter within the balustrade"). The very term used to exclude Samaritans from the temple is now used to identify the only one who truly worships.

12. **17:19 — "your faith has saved you" (η πιστις σου σεσωκεν σε):** Sinaiticus reads η πιστις σου σεσωκεν σε. The perfect tense σεσωκεν ("has saved") indicates a completed action with ongoing results. The verb σωζω encompasses both physical healing and spiritual salvation — all ten were "cleansed" (εκαθαρισθησαν), but only the Samaritan is told he has been "saved" (σεσωκεν). The distinction is crucial: cleansing (καθαρισις) is physical restoration; salvation (σωτηρια) is something more. The nine received healing; the Samaritan, by returning in faith and gratitude, received salvation. His faith was demonstrated not merely in obeying the command to go (all ten did that) but in turning back — recognizing that the healer himself, not merely the healing, is the true gift.

13. **17:20-21 — "The kingdom of God does not come with observation" (ουκ ερχεται η βασιλεια του θεου μετα παρατηρησεως):** Sinaiticus reads ουκ ερχεται η βασιλεια του θεου μετα παρατηρησεως. The word παρατηρησις ("observation/watching") appears only here in the New Testament, derived from παρατηρεω ("to watch closely/observe carefully"), used of Pharisees watching Jesus (6:7; 14:1; 20:20). The kingdom does not arrive in a manner that can be detected by careful, calculating observation — it is not an event on a timeline that can be predicted or tracked. The phrase εντος υμων ("within you" or "among you/in your midst") is debated: εντος can mean "inside" (interiority) or "in the midst of" (presence). Given that Jesus addresses Pharisees, "in your midst" may be primary — the kingdom is present in the person of Jesus standing before them — though the interior dimension cannot be excluded.

14. **17:22 — "you will desire to see one of the days of the Son of Man" (επιθυμησετε μιαν των ημερων του υιου του ανθρωπου ιδειν):** Sinaiticus reads επιθυμησετε μιαν των ημερων του υιου του ανθρωπου ιδειν. The verb επιθυμεω ("to desire/long for") indicates intense yearning. The phrase "one of the days" (μιαν των ημερων) suggests that the "days of the Son of Man" constitute a period, and the disciples will long to experience even a single day of it — perhaps looking back on the present time of Jesus' earthly presence, or forward to his glorious return. The stark conclusion "and you will not see [it]" (και ουκ οψεσθε) warns of a coming period of absence when the Son of Man will not be visibly present, and the longing will be unfulfilled — the inter-advent period of patient waiting.

15. **17:24 — "the lightning, flashing" (η αστραπη αστραπτουσα):** Sinaiticus reads η αστραπη αστραπτουσα — a cognate construction (the lightning lightnings/flashes) emphasizing the brilliance and instantaneity of the event. The point of comparison is visibility and universality: lightning does not appear in one corner — it illuminates the entire sky from one horizon to the other. So the Son of Man's coming will not be a local, hidden, debatable event that must be sought out (contrary to the false reports of verse 23) but a universal, unmistakable, instantaneous manifestation. The phrase εν τη ημερα αυτου ("in his day") — present in Sinaiticus — specifies a particular day, the day of his revelation.

16. **17:25 — "But first it is necessary [for] him to suffer many [things]" (πρωτον δε δει αυτον πολλα παθειν):** Sinaiticus reads πρωτον δε δει αυτον πολλα παθειν. The word δει ("it is necessary") expresses divine necessity — not mere circumstantial inevitability but the determined will and plan of God (cf. 9:22; 24:7, 26, 44). Before the glorious manifestation described in verse 24, the Son of Man must first (πρωτον) suffer and be rejected (αποδοκιμασθηναι, "to be rejected after testing" — the word used for rejecting a building stone as unfit, cf. 20:17; 1 Peter 2:4, 7). The phrase απο της γενεας ταυτης ("by this generation") identifies the present generation as the agent of rejection — the very people witnessing his ministry.

17. **17:26-27 — "as it came-to-be in the days of Noah" (καθως εγενετο εν ταις ημεραις Νωε):** Sinaiticus reads καθως εγενετο εν ταις ημεραις Νωε. The comparison is not primarily about the wickedness of Noah's generation but about its normalcy — they were eating (ησθιον), drinking (επινον), marrying (εγαμουν), being-given-in-marriage (εγαμιζοντο) — all imperfect tenses indicating ongoing, habitual activity. Life continued in its ordinary rhythms right up to the day (αχρι ης ημερας) Noah entered the ark. The flood came (ηλθεν ο κατακλυσμος) and "destroyed all" (απωλεσεν παντας/απαντας). The word κατακλυσμος ("flood/deluge") appears only here and in Matthew 24:38-39 in the New Testament (cf. Genesis 6-8 LXX; 2 Peter 2:5). The warning is about complacent unpreparedness — not extraordinary wickedness but ordinary obliviousness to approaching judgment.

18. **17:28-29 — "in the days of Lot" (εν ταις ημεραις Λωτ):** Sinaiticus reads εν ταις ημεραις Λωτ. The Lot comparison, unique to Luke, extends the list of normal activities: eating, drinking, buying (ηγοραζον), selling (επωλουν), planting (εφυτευον), building (ωκοδομουν) — six activities (compared to Noah's four), all in the imperfect tense, all utterly ordinary. The verb εβρεξεν ("it rained") takes πυρ και θειον ("fire and brimstone/sulfur") as its object — God rained down destruction from heaven (απ ουρανου), echoing Genesis 19:24 LXX. The juxtaposition of mundane human activity and sudden divine destruction is the rhetorical point: the ordinary conceals the approaching catastrophic.

19. **17:31 — "let him not come-down to take them up" (μη καταβατω αραι αυτα):** Sinaiticus reads μη καταβατω αραι αυτα. The flat-roofed Palestinian house had an external staircase; one could flee from the rooftop without entering the house. The command not to go back for possessions (σκευη, "goods/vessels/equipment") echoes the urgency of Lot's escape from Sodom (Genesis 19:17, "do not look behind you"). The parallel command to the one in the field — μη επιστρεψατω εις τα οπισω ("let him not turn to the things behind") — uses the same language as Lot's wife's fatal backward glance, directly setting up verse 32.

20. **17:32 — "Remember the wife of Lot" (μνημονευετε της γυναικος Λωτ):** Sinaiticus reads μνημονευετε της γυναικος Λωτ. This is the shortest verse in Luke's Gospel — three words in Greek — and one of the most devastating. The present imperative μνημονευετε ("keep remembering") commands ongoing, habitual remembrance. Lot's wife (Genesis 19:26) looked back toward Sodom and became a pillar of salt. Her sin was not mere curiosity but divided loyalty — her body moved forward toward salvation while her heart turned back toward the condemned city. She is the embodiment of the warning in verse 31: the person who, in the moment of crisis, cannot let go of what is behind. She is the anti-type of the Samaritan leper who turned back to Jesus, and the type of the nine who continued forward to the priests without returning.

21. **17:33 — "Whoever seeks to preserve his soul will lose it" (ος εαν ζητηση την ψυχην αυτου περιποιησασθαι απολεσει αυτην):** Sinaiticus reads ος εαν ζητηση την ψυχην αυτου περιποιησασθαι απολεσει αυτην. The verb περιποιεομαι ("to preserve/acquire/save for oneself") is middle voice — the emphasis is on self-interested preservation. The paradox is stated without qualification: the one who grasps at life (ψυχη, "soul/life") loses it; the one who loses (απολεση) it will ζωογονησει ("bring-it-to-life/generate-life-in-it"). The verb ζωογονεω (ζωος + γονος, "living" + "generation") literally means "to produce alive" — used in the LXX for preserving life (Exodus 1:17-18, the midwives who let the Hebrew boys live). The paradox resolves only eschatologically: what looks like loss in the present age is life-generation in the age to come.

22. **17:34-35 — "two upon one bed...two grinding together":** Sinaiticus reads δυο επι κλινης μιας ("two upon one bed") and δυο εσονται αληθουσαι επι το αυτο ("two will be grinding together"). The scenes depict nighttime (bed) and daytime (grinding grain) activity — the coming of the Son of Man is unpredictable as to timing, catching people in their ordinary routines. The verb παραλημφθησεται ("will be taken-along") and αφεθησεται ("will be left") indicate sudden separation of those who are intimately connected — sharing a bed, sharing a millstone. The passive voice indicates divine agency: God takes and God leaves. The discrimination is individual, not collective — proximity to the faithful does not guarantee inclusion.

23. **17:36 — Verse absent from Sinaiticus:** Codex Sinaiticus omits verse 36 entirely ("Two [men] will be in the field; the one will be taken-along and the other left"), which is found in later manuscripts and appears to be an assimilation from Matthew 24:40. The verse is absent from the earliest and most reliable witnesses (Sinaiticus, Vaticanus, P75) and is regarded by modern text-critical scholarship as a secondary addition. EMET follows the Sinaiticus reading.

24. **17:37 — "Where the body [is], there also the eagles will be gathered-together" (οπου το σωμα, εκει και οι αετοι επισυναχθησονται):** Sinaiticus reads οπου το σωμα, εκει και οι αετοι επισυναχθησονται. The disciples' question "Where, Lord?" (που, κυριε;) seeks geographical specificity — where will this separation occur? Jesus answers with a proverb that refuses the question's terms. The word σωμα ("body") — rather than πτωμα ("corpse") as in Matthew 24:28 — may be significant, though both can refer to a dead body. The αετοι ("eagles") are more likely vultures (the griffon vulture, Gyps fulvens, was common in Palestine and was called αετος in Greek); eagles do not typically feed on carrion, but vultures do. The proverb means: just as vultures inevitably gather where a carcass lies — requiring no announcement, no searching — so the coming judgment will be self-evident, unmistakable, and inescapable. The point is not location but inevitability.

25. **17:1-37 — The structure of Luke 17:** The chapter divides into four sections united by the theme of faith in the inter-advent period: (1) Teaching on stumbling-blocks and forgiveness (vv. 1-4) — communal ethics. (2) Teaching on faith and duty (vv. 5-10) — the nature of faith and service. (3) The ten lepers (vv. 11-19) — faith enacted, with the Samaritan as model. (4) The coming of the kingdom and the Son of Man (vv. 20-37) — eschatological readiness. The chapter moves from the intimate (brother-to-brother forgiveness) to the cosmic (the day of the Son of Man), but the connecting thread is faith: faith that forgives (vv. 3-4), faith the size of a mustard seed (v. 6), faith that saves (v. 19), and the implied question of 18:8 — "when the Son of Man comes, will he find faith on the earth?" The Samaritan leper stands at the chapter's center as the embodiment of genuine faith: he obeyed, he was healed, he returned, he worshipped, and he was saved.
